summary: Justin Brennan coaches John through underwriting a 16-unit multifamily property in South Dakota using the deal analyzer, demonstrating how to verify broker assumptions, back into realistic numbers that meet investor return metrics (15% IRR, 1.8x equity multiple, 6-10% cash-on-cash), and negotiate with brokers. He also reviews Michael's pitch deck for a 30-unit Louisville deal to show the capital-raise phase workflow.
takeaways: Use the income approach formula (NOI ÷ cap rate = value) to verify asking prices; don't trust broker offering memorandums
Back into purchase price from required metrics: 15% IRR minimum, 1.8x+ equity multiple, 6-10% cash-on-cash return, 1.25+ debt service coverage ratio
Set maintenance/repairs reserves at $300/unit/month (~$4,800/year for 16-unit) to avoid under-capitalization and future capital calls to investors
For value-add renovations, require $200-$300/month rent bump per unit to justify $5,000-$10,000/unit capex spend (return on cost analysis)
Use 8-10% vacancy & credit loss assumption (not broker's 5%) plus realistic operating expense ratio of 48-50% for conservative underwriting
Verify broker NOI against actual 2-year bank statements and P&L, not potential income; request proof of collected revenue
Use fully amortized debt service (not interest-only) for bank underwriting, even if seller offers IO terms initially
Pitch deck must answer 90% of investor questions: for $25K/$50K/$100K investment, what returns and timeline; include market comparables, team, cash flow projections, preferred return structure

skill_candidates.change: Apply income approach formula rigorously instead of relying on broker cap rates; verify all three variables (value, NOI, cap rate) using basic algebra
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin caught broker math error ($84,153 NOI ÷ 6.32% cap ≠ $1.45M value) and showed correct calculation yields $1.33M; emphasized 'trusting and verifying' broker OMs because they fluff numbers

skill_candidates.change: Build conservative expense models using actual operating history, not broker projections; default to 8-10% vacancy and $300/unit/month reserves
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin showed how 5% vacancy assumption and low maintenance estimates ($9.5K) were unrealistic; bumped maintenance to $12K and vacancy to 8% to reflect actual market conditions

skill_candidates.change: Back all deals into three minimum metrics before pursuing: 15% IRR, 1.8x equity multiple, 1.25+ DCR; reject deals that miss these thresholds
skill_candidates.evidence: John's South Dakota deal showed 13% IRR and failed DCR at $1.25M ask; Justin walked him through reducing offer to $1M to hit required metrics, demonstrating deal fails if numbers don't align

skill_candidates.change: Create pitch deck as lead-generation tool attached to landing page CRM workflow; enables sending link to prospects and capturing contact info automatically
skill_candidates.evidence: Michael's 30-unit pitch deck reviewed end-to-end; Justin showed how landing page + PDF download creates leads in CRM without manual data entry, allowing follow-up at restaurants/banks

title: Weekly Coaching_10-09-25 | Justin Brennan
